FITNESS · LEASE · MAY 2026

Gym lease negotiation.

The single most expensive document a first-time gym owner signs. The lease decisions made in 30 days lock in 10 years of cost structure — and the items most operators don't negotiate are usually the items the landlord is most willing to give. Here are the seven highest-leverage negotiation points, the traps to avoid, and the language to push back on.

The honest frame

Landlords negotiate; first-timers usually don't

Commercial landlords negotiate leases professionally; first-time gym owners negotiate one in their lifetime. The asymmetry is brutal. The first lease term offered is almost never the best the landlord will agree to — and getting to the better terms requires either professional representation (broker + attorney) or unusual patience.

Always engage: a commercial real estate attorney ($2K-$6K for a single-tenant retail lease) and a tenant-side broker (paid by landlord, costs you nothing). The cost of professional representation pays back through a single negotiated TI bump or personal-guarantee carve-out.

The seven big items

Where the real money lives

1. TI (tenant improvement) allowance

The single most-impactful number in the lease. TI is landlord-funded buildout capital amortized into rent.

  • Typical: $20-$40/sq ft for second-gen retail in Tier-2/3 markets. $40-$80/sq ft in Tier-1 or for first-gen/ground-up.
  • For a 4,000 sq ft boutique: Going from $25/sq ft to $45/sq ft = $80K of effectively-free capital.
  • Negotiate by trading: longer lease term (10 years vs 5+5), stronger personal guarantee, better credit story.

2. Rent abatement (free rent during buildout)

  • Typical: 4-6 months of free base rent for second-gen; 6-9 months for ground-up/heavy buildout.
  • You still pay: CAM, utilities, insurance, real estate tax pass-throughs.
  • Push for: abatement that starts at lease execution, not at certificate of occupancy.

3. Base rent + annual escalators

  • Typical: Year 1 base + 2-3% annual escalator. Push back on any landlord asking for 4%+ or CPI-uncapped.
  • Cap CPI-based escalators at 3% if CPI-pegged is unavoidable.
  • Watch for: "fair market value" reset clauses at year 5 or 7 — these can balloon rent at a horrible moment.

4. CAM and operating expense caps

  • What CAM is: Common Area Maintenance — your pro-rata share of landlord's costs for the building/center.
  • Cap the annual increase: 5-7% year-over-year max, or push for a fixed CAM that doesn't increase.
  • Exclude: capital expenditures, landlord's leasing commissions, costs related to other tenants' improvements.
  • Audit rights: insist on the right to audit CAM charges annually. Landlords occasionally err in their own favor.

5. Personal guarantee scope

Almost always required from first-time operators. But scope is negotiable.

  • Push for limited recourse: Capped at 12-18 months of base rent.
  • Burn-off clause: Guarantee reduces or eliminates after years 2 or 3 if rent is paid on time.
  • "Good guy" guarantee: Limits your liability if you give written notice and vacate by a specified date. Common and reasonable.
  • Joint and several with spouse: Decline. Sign as the LLC owner only, not as your spouse too.

6. Use clause + assignment + sublease

  • Use clause: Make it broad. "Fitness center and related uses" beats "CrossFit affiliate" — gives you flexibility if you pivot the format.
  • Assignment rights: Right to assign the lease to a buyer if you sell the business. Critical to exit value.
  • Sublease rights: Right to sublease unused space (e.g., a personal training studio inside a larger box).
  • Landlord consent: "Not to be unreasonably withheld" — push for this language on all consents.

7. Exit clauses

  • Kickout rights: Right to terminate at year 3 or 5 if revenue thresholds aren't met. Hard to get in strong markets, possible in weak ones.
  • Co-tenancy clauses: Right to reduced rent or termination if anchor tenants leave the center (huge in retail centers where Whole Foods or grocery anchor matters).
  • Casualty / condemnation: Clear language on termination rights if the property is damaged.
The math

What good negotiation actually saves

Worked example: 4,000 sq ft boutique studio, 10-year lease at $35/sq ft NNN base rent.

ItemFirst offerWell-negotiated10-year impact
TI allowance$25/sq ft = $100K$45/sq ft = $180K+$80K (essentially free capital)
Rent abatement3 months5 months+$23K (avoided rent)
Annual escalator3.5%2.5%~$25K (over 10 yrs)
CAM capNone5%/year cap~$15-$40K (depends on landlord behavior)
Personal guaranteeUnlimited, full term18-month rolling, burns off year 4Materially lower personal risk
Total cash + risk impact~$140-$170K over lease term

The pro-rep ROI: A $4K-$6K attorney + tenant broker pair usually negotiates 5-10x their cost in lease improvements. Skipping this is the single most-expensive false economy in opening a gym.

Traps

Five lease traps to push back on

  • 1. Relocation clauses. "Landlord may relocate tenant to comparable space" — never sign this. Your members built drive-time habits around your address.
  • 2. Exclusive use clauses (yours and theirs). Demand exclusivity in your use category (no other fitness tenant in the center). Push back on the landlord's exclusivity restrictions that limit your offerings.
  • 3. Percentage rent. Common in retail center leases — extra rent if revenue exceeds a threshold. Decline or set the threshold very high.
  • 4. Estoppel + SNDA on landlord-favorable terms. Required by lenders; sign with attorney review.
  • 5. Annual statement audit limitations. Some leases limit your right to audit CAM. Reject — you need the right to verify charges.
Companion resources
